title.en: Caste, Income and Domicile Certificates — Who Issues Them, Documents and Timelines

answerBox.en: Caste, income and domicile (residence) certificates are issued by your tehsil/SDM office — applied online through state e-district portals in almost every state. Typical requirements: ID and address proof, a sworn affidavit, and for caste certificates, proof of a relative's prior certificate or community records. Fees are nominal (₹20–100); statutory timelines run 7–30 days under state service-guarantee laws.

en.label: Statutory issue timeline
en.startsFrom: state Right to Public Services Acts

en.label: Income certificate validity
en.startsFrom: issue date; renew per scheme rules

en.title: Know which certificate and which authority
en.summary: Tehsildar/SDM issues all three; formats differ by purpose.
en.detail: **Caste certificate** (SC/ST/OBC — and the central OBC **non-creamy-layer** format for central jobs/institutes), **income certificate** (family income for scholarships, EWS, fee waivers), and **domicile/residence certificate** (state quotas, admissions) are all issued by the **tehsildar/SDM/revenue office** of your residence. Central-format OBC-NCL and EWS certificates have prescribed proformas — name the exact purpose in the application so the right format issues.

en.title: Apply online on your state portal
en.summary: e-district portals cover all three; track by application number.
en.detail: Every major state runs these through its e-district/citizen portal (linked from **[india.gov.in](https://www.india.gov.in)** service directory) — create the account, pick the certificate service, fill the family/income/residence details, upload documents and pay the **₹20–100** fee. Documents commonly needed: Aadhaar, address proof, ration card, passport photo, **supporting affidavit** (our ₹199 drafting covers the state format), plus salary slips/ITR for income and old caste records for caste.

en.title: Caste certificates: the lineage evidence
en.summary: A relative's certificate or old records anchor verification.
en.detail: Verification for caste certificates leans on **lineage**: a parent's/grandparent's caste certificate, old school records (TC showing caste), land/revenue records, or community verification by the patwari. Migrant families are certified by the state of **origin's** records — a person's caste status doesn't change by migration, but which state issues (and whether the new state recognises it for its quotas) follows origin-state rules. OBC **non-creamy-layer** additionally screens parental income/office — renewed as income changes.

en.title: Chase timelines with the service-guarantee law
en.summary: RTPS Acts fix deadlines — and appeal officers exist.
en.detail: Most states' **Right to Public Services Acts** notify deadlines for these certificates (commonly **7–30 days**) with appellate officers empowered to penalise defaulting officials. If your application sits: file the RTPS appeal citing the notified timeline, and an **[RTI application](/help/government-paperwork/rti-application-how-to)** asking the status and officer holding the file — the named-officer effect moves stuck files. Renew income certificates per scheme rules (commonly valid 6–12 months); caste and domicile certificates generally don't expire.

en.footnote: False caste/income declarations invite prosecution and cancellation of everything obtained — benefits, admissions, jobs — with recovery. These certificates are checked against records; apply on truth, appeal on delay.

en.q: How long is an income certificate valid?
en.a: Commonly 6 months to 1 year from issue (state-wise), because income changes annually — scholarship and EWS schemes state the freshness they need. Caste certificates are generally lifetime documents; OBC non-creamy-layer status needs periodic renewal since it depends on current parental income.

en.q: My father never made a caste certificate. Can I still get one?
en.a: Yes — lineage can be proved through other records: grandparents' certificates, old school registers recording caste, revenue records, or community/panchayat verification. The enquiry takes longer without a direct parental certificate, but absence of one is not a bar.

en.q: I moved states. Which state issues my certificates?
en.a: Domicile follows genuine residence per the new state's rules (period-of-residence conditions apply). Caste follows the family's state of origin — the origin state certifies, and central institutions accept it; state-quota recognition in the new state depends on that state's policy. Income certificates issue where you reside.

en.q: The application has been pending for months. What actually works?
en.a: Two levers: the state Right to Public Services appeal (cites the notified deadline; officers face fines) and an RTI asking the file's status and the officer responsible. Both convert quiet delay into recorded accountability — most stuck certificates issue within weeks of that combination.

en.q: What is the EWS certificate and who qualifies?
en.a: The Economically Weaker Section certificate (10% quota for non-SC/ST/OBC) requires family income below ₹8 lakh and asset ceilings (land/house limits) — issued by the same tehsil/SDM machinery on the central proforma, valid for the financial year. Income + asset affidavits and verification apply.

reviewer: Adv. Priya Sharma, Bar Council of Delhi, D/1284/2016
author: WakilBhai Editorial Team
